title: OpenAI wants its new tool to do your work for you and with you
gist: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, an agentic tool that can run multi-hour workflows, connect to enterprise apps, access desktop files, and merge in Codex, alongside a new GPT-5.6 model, while sunsetting the Atlas browser and warning of high token-consumption costs.

insights.claim: ChatGPT Work can stay with a project for hours and turn a goal into finished work, solving the prior limitation where Atlas Agent Mode tasks stopped after a few minutes.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Long-horizon autonomous execution is the key threshold for agents to displace real knowledge work; a genuine step here raises the stakes for productivity-software incumbents.
insights.quote: stay with a project for hours if needed, and turn a goal into finished work
insights.locator: Last year, when we tested out the Agent Mode

insights.claim: ChatGPT Work connects to Slack, Microsoft Teams, Google Drive, and SharePoint via plugins, can access and modify desktop files, and use a built-in browser, with an updated Chrome extension for web-native tasks.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Deep integration into core workplace apps is OpenAI's move to become the workflow layer, a direct competitive threat to Microsoft Copilot and Google.
insights.locator: ChatGPT Work can connect to common workplace management tools

insights.claim: OpenAI is sunsetting its dedicated Atlas web browser less than nine months after launch, folding web tasks into the Chrome extension and ChatGPT Work.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: A rapid product retirement signals OpenAI consolidating around the agent surface rather than a standalone browser, a strategy pivot worth noting.
insights.locator: OpenAI says its dedicated Atlas web browser is now being sunset

insights.claim: OpenAI is merging its coding-focused Codex app into ChatGPT Work while keeping Codex as a separate view, and rebranding the existing desktop app as deprecated 'ChatGPT Classic.'
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Consolidating chat, work, and coding into one billable surface pushes users toward higher-consumption tiers.

insights.claim: ChatGPT Work is billed like Codex with plans up to $100/month using a credit system, and OpenAI warns complex tasks may consume significantly more of a plan's usage, prompting spend limits for enterprise/Edu.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Agent 'sticker shock' from token consumption is a real adoption friction and a margin/monetization lever OpenAI is actively managing.
insights.quote: designed for longer, more involved work than a typical chat request
insights.locator: These distinctions between chat and work could be important

insights.claim: The new GPT-5.6 model operates in three tiers, with the most expensive costing $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens, promising 'stronger performance per dollar.'
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Explicit token pricing sets a benchmark for comparing frontier-model cost-efficiency across providers.
insights.locator: The ChatGPT Work rollout comes alongside the debut
